paragraphs: Class Rotation, Variety, and Balance Rules Specification
DOC-14 | Version 1.0 | Meal Planning RE Technical Docs v1
1. Purpose and Scope
Defines rotation, variety, heaviness, repeat tolerance, weekday/weekend, meal-slot, and coherence rules for realistic weekly meal-class plans.

This document is part of the canonical 28-document technical system. It should be read in the order defined in DOC-00. It is written for an AI coding agent that must implement the agreed recommendation engine exactly, without inventing a competing architecture.
2. Applicable Build Series
3. Core Implementation Invariants
Always build a household profile before generating any plan.
Always map user-facing cohort answers into backend personas and overlays; do not expose all backend personas in onboarding.
Always generate a 7-day class-first plan before selecting dishes.
Always keep primary family meal classes separate from member-specific add-on components.
Never allow kid, elderly, pregnancy, postpartum, diabetic, or recovery add-on classes to replace the main family meal unless the household itself has only that member segment.
Always apply hard constraints before scoring: diet type, allergies, Jain/fasting rules, and Never list.
Always use home state and current city as separate signals; do not collapse them into one location field.
Always use class-to-dish mapping keyed by meal_class_code; never mix dishes from unrelated meal classes.
Use v3 workbook IDs as canonical seed IDs unless a later approved source-data version supersedes them.
4. Why Variety Rules Exist

Indian meal weeks have stable patterns but not random dish chaos. Users repeat classes such as dal-sabzi-roti, rice-dal, poha/upma, simple sabzi, fish curry rice, or khichdi, but they expect sensible spacing and weekend shifts. The engine should balance familiarity and freshness.
5. Default Rotation Rules
6. Meal-Slot Specific Logic
Breakfast can repeat more than lunch/dinner for many Indian households.
Lunch is often the most stable home-style meal: dal, sabzi, roti, rice, fish curry, curd rice, etc.
Dinner is often lighter or simpler on weekdays and more special on weekends.
Snacks vary strongly by kids, office, fitness, and city lifestyle.
Weekend plans can introduce richer regional specials, fried breakfast, non-veg, sweets, or elaborate meals.
7. Heaviness and Coherence

Coherence sensitivity should be learned over time. Before learning, use gentle defaults: working urban families often prefer balanced weekday heaviness; elderly and health-conscious households lean lighter; young high-calorie households may tolerate heavy meals more often. The planner should not force everyone into diet-style balance.
8. Validation Algorithm
Count primary class occurrences by slot and week.
Count heavy classes per day.
Check consecutive heavy meals.
Check weekend special allocation.
Check state/city overlay representation.
Check add-on presence for required member segments.
If violation occurs, replace using secondary/tertiary class or safe fallback.
9. AI Coding Agent Instructions
Read DOC-00 first to understand build sequence and document dependencies.
Use this document when implementing: BUILD-04.
Treat all IDs in source workbook v3 as canonical seed data identifiers.
When logic and data conflict, follow the rule hierarchy: hard constraints > add-on separation > weekly class matrix > state/city overlay > dish scoring > randomization.
Do not create new meal class codes, persona codes, or enum values in application code. Additions must go through DOC-27 Admin CMS and DOC-28 governance.
Write unit tests based on the acceptance criteria in this document and DOC-25.
10. Acceptance Criteria
Weekly plan feels realistic, not random.
Class repetition is allowed when behaviorally normal but controlled.
Weekend meals differ from weekdays in appropriate cohorts.
11. Change Log

Generate meal classes first; expand to dishes second; attach member add-ons separately.
Generate 7-day meal-class plan from cohort matrix, regional rules, city overlay, weekday/weekend rhythm, variety rules.
Max 3 times/week unless high-repeat breakfast persona.
Max 3 times/week; dal/sabzi staples can repeat with dish variation.
Max 2 times/week unless light dinner persona.
Avoid heavy breakfast + heavy lunch + heavy dinner for normal coherence users.
Allow 1-3 richer/fried/non-veg/festive classes where cohort permits.
Initial canonical technical document generated from agreed v3 workbook and documentation architecture.
